findings.description: Heading alignment and typography differ from the PDF.
findings.evidence: On page 1, the PDF title block is centered with smaller serif text for 'FACT SHEET' and the permit title, while the HTML uses larger sans-serif text and different spacing/alignment, making the heading hierarchy look somewhat different though still clear.

findings.description: HTML contains fabricated EPA ID number text not present in the PDF.
findings.evidence: PDF header shows: "EPA ID Number: NCD 045 646 924" and body text says documents may be located using "EPA ID Number: NCD049773245." HTML header matches the first ID, but the body text changes this to "Documents may be located using the EPA ID Number: NCD049773245." The body ID in HTML does not appear in the PDF as shown.

findings.description: HTML fabricates link presentation by turning a plain-text URL reference into a hyperlink-like URL string with altered surrounding text.
findings.evidence: PDF states: "The draft permit and all data submitted by the applicant is available as part of the administrative record at the https://deq.nc.gov/about/divisions/waste-management/laserfiche." HTML reproduces the URL as plain text, but adds article wording "at the https://..." and preserves it as web content without confirming actual hyperlink metadata from the PDF. More importantly, this same sentence is paired with the fabricated EPA ID in the next sentence.

findings.description: Heading hierarchy is inaccurate and structurally broken.
findings.evidence: PDF visually has a top title "FACT SHEET" and centered subtitle block "Draft Corrective Action Permit for Kaba Ilco Corporation..." HTML uses a div for "FACT SHEET" instead of a heading, then starts with <h2> "Draft Corrective Action Permit" and has no h1. It also repeats page-level header structure with multiple <main> elements, which does not reflect a clean document heading hierarchy.

findings.description: Text accuracy error changes meaning in the site history/remediation paragraph.
findings.evidence: PDF says: "A reinforced concrete was installed." HTML also says "A reinforced concrete was installed." This phrase appears likely OCR-derived and incomplete, but because the source PDF itself shows the same text, this is not an HTML error. However, the HTML changes the sentence introducing the permit availability by adding "the" before the URL and alters the EPA ID sentence to a different identifier, which changes factual meaning.
